turns.content: Imagine we are building a configuration parser for our new microservice framework where every field must be explicitly set. We want to avoid runtime errors by ensuring fields are present at compile time instead of relying on default values that might get missed during deployment.

That sounds like an excellent use case for a custom derive macro rather than manual boilerplate. Instead of writing repetitive code, you could implement a proc-macro that wraps your struct definition and automatically generates a Builder pattern with required fields marked as mandatory using the `required()` attribute syntax. This approach leverages Rust's type system to enforce constraints at compile time, which is significantly faster than runtime validation.

The problem is we already have a robust library for serialization using serde_json::Value and want our builder pattern to integrate seamlessly without duplicating logic or creating separate types for configuration versus data. How do we ensure the generated builder plays nicely with that existing ecosystem?

You can achieve this by having your custom derive macro generate code that implements the `Clone` trait and derives from a base struct already compatible with serde's serialization traits. The macro will produce a Builder type that holds an internal state, allowing you to chain `.field()` calls while maintaining full compatibility with `serde_json::Value`. You can even implement a trait like `IntoConfig` so that partial builders can be converted into the final configuration object seamlessly.

But I'm concerned about the complexity of writing the macro itself. If we make an error in how the fields are generated, does it break our entire build pipeline? What happens if someone uses this builder and forgets a required field at runtime after compilation has passed?

The beauty of this approach is that the compiler will catch missing required fields before your code ever runs. If a user forgets to call `.field()` on a mandatory attribute, the build process fails immediately with a clear error message pointing to the exact line where the field was omitted. This prevents any possibility of runtime failures due to uninitialized data because the type system guarantees that all required fields must be provided at compile time.

I see how that helps, but there's still a risk if we're integrating with external crates like tokio or actix-web where configuration objects are often passed through async contexts. Can this builder pattern handle those scenarios without requiring manual trait implementations for every single library?

Not at all. Since your custom derive macro generates code that implements standard Rust traits like `Clone` and potentially a generic `FromBuilder<T>` trait, it works seamlessly with any crate that expects these patterns. You don't need to manually implement traits for tokio::runtime or actix_web::middleware; the generated Builder type will naturally satisfy their expectations because they rely on standard serialization and cloning interfaces.

Okay, so the main advantage is catching errors early, but what if we want optional fields too? Can this same macro handle both required and optional attributes in a single pass without needing separate macros or complex conditional logic inside the proc-macro itself?

Absolutely. Your custom derive can inspect each field's attribute to determine whether it should be required or optional. For required fields, it generates mandatory calls like `.field()`, while for optional ones marked with `optional()`, it adds a method that accepts an Option<T>. This allows you to define complex configurations in one go without needing multiple macros.

I keep getting confused about why a simple derive macro like serde would just magically create all these builder methods when I only have a struct definition. It feels like magic to me since I'm used to manually writing code.

It isn't actually magic, it's Rust's proc-macro system working under the hood. When you use #[derive], the compiler runs your attribute macro at compile time which parses your struct and generates the builder implementation for you using traits like Builder or IntoBuilder.
Oh, okay so I'm supposed to write that custom derive code myself? That sounds tedious if every struct needs it. Can't I just use something standard?

You can use crates like serde or builder-pattern macros, but for advanced control over required vs optional fields and compile-time error messages, you often need a full proc macro implementation rather than just using an existing crate.
Wait, so I'm thinking the wrong thing. I thought I could just add #[derive] to my struct and it would handle everything automatically without me writing any procedural code at all.
That is exactly the assumption you made which leads to frustration later when you need specific error messages or builder logic that standard crates don't provide by default.
Can you show me how to actually implement this full proc macro so I understand what's happening instead of just trusting it works?

Sure, you would write a custom derive macro in Rust using the syn and quote crates from the ecosystem. The macro receives your struct definition via syn::parse_derive_input, then generates code using quote::quote! that implements a Builder trait with required fields enforcing compile-time checks.

I'm building a proof-of-concept to evaluate this custom derive macro thing I heard about. It feels like the builder pattern I used in my previous job with the Builder trait, but instead of manually calling methods on a struct, it looks like the macro generates all that boilerplate for me.

That's exactly right. You are moving from manual implementation using traits and structs to a proc-macro approach where the compiler runs code at compile time to generate your Builder struct and its methods automatically based on your input attributes.

I think I get it, but I'm making a mistake. I assume that if I just add #[derive(Builder)] to my DataStruct, the macro will handle everything and give me a fully working builder with all fields optional by default so I don't have to write any extra code.

That is a common mistake beginners make. The derive macro does not automatically make every field optional; you must explicitly define which fields are required and which are optional in your attribute syntax before the macro can generate the correct builder logic.
Oh, okay. So I have to manually specify #[builder(required = "id", optional = "name")] instead of just using a blanket derive? That sounds like extra work compared to what I was expecting.

Yes, you must be explicit. The macro inspects your attribute list to generate the builder methods and validation logic. If you omit required fields from that list, the generated code will fail at compile time because it cannot guarantee those values are present.
So if I forget to mark a field as required in the attribute, the build process stops and gives me a compile-time error? That's actually better than runtime errors where I'd get a null pointer later.

Exactly. It is a compile-time error that prevents your binary from being built until you fix the missing required field in the attribute list, which saves you from debugging logic errors down the line.
That makes sense. But what if I have nested structs? Does this macro handle recursive builders for those inner types automatically?

The macro does support nested structures, but you still need to ensure every field in the entire hierarchy is correctly marked as required or optional. The generated builder will recursively call into the child structs' builders.
Got it. One last thing, how do I verify that the macro actually ran and generated the code? Can't I just comment out the derive?
You can use cargo expand to see what the proc-macro generates, or you can check your build output for warnings if something goes wrong. The macro itself does not produce runtime logs.
